coredata.dc:description: The biotoxicity and environmental applications of carbon nanomaterials have always been the focus of research. In this research, functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) show high promotion effects on regulating the growth, development and yield of tomato under drought stress, due to their up-regulation effects on the physiological processes of plants including photosynthesis, antioxidant system, osmotic adjustment, as well as soil amelioration in physicochemical properties and microbial environment during vegetative and reproductive growth stage. The reduction of tissue water content and water use efficiency are moderated by FCNs through improving root vigor and osmolytes (soluble sugar and proline) level, which contributes to maintain the enzyme function, photosynthesis and nutrient uptake in plant. FCNs regulate the enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant system to scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) and inhibit the lipid peroxidation, thus protect the membrane structure and function of plant cells under stress. FCNs up-regulate soil microbial communities under drought stress by regulating the soil pH, enzyme activity, organic carbon and organic matters contents. Our results prove that FCNs are biological friendly to plant growth and soil environment under drought stress, thus exhibit potential as emendator to promote plant tolerance and improve agricultural productivity in water-deficient areas.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM and HRTEM photos (a), size distribution (b), Raman spectrum (c), FTIR spectrum (d), XPS survey spectrum (e), C1s XPS spectrum (f) and O1s XPS spectrum (g) of FCNs used in this study. The size distribution in (b) is calculated randomly from (a).

Dosage effect of FCNs on tomato seedlings. (a): Dry biomass of tomato seedlings. (b–e): Enzymes activities of leaves. (f–h): GSH and GSSH levels in leaves. (i): MDA contents in leaves. Data are taken in triplicate and reported as the mean ± SD. Different lowercase letters on bars indicate the significant differences among treatments at p ≤ 0.05.

FCNs regulate vegetative growth and physiology of tomato plant under drought stress. (a): Leaves number of tomato plant (n = 5). (b): Biomass of plant (n = 5). (c): Root activity of seedling. (d): Water content of tomato plant. (e): Total chlorophyll level. (f): MDA content in leaf after 15 days of stress drought. (g–j): Antioxidant enzyme activity in root and leaf after 15 days of stress drought. Values in (c–j) are taken in triplicate. Data reported is the mean ± SD. Different lowercase letters on bars indicate the significant differences among treatments at p ≤ 0.05.

FCNs regulate reproductive growth and development of tomato plant under long-term drought stress. (a, b): Flower number of tomato plant under drought stress for 35 days. (c, d): Fruit number and yield of tomato under drought stress for 45 days. (e): Soluble sugar contents in tomato leaves and fruits after 45 days of drought stress. (f): WUE of tomato plant under drought stress for 45 days. Data are taken in triplicate (n = 3) and reported as the mean ± SD. Different lowercase letters on bars indicate the significant differences among treatments at p ≤ 0.05.

FCNs influence the antioxidant system of tomato plant at reproductive stage under long-term drought stress. (a–d): Enzyme activities of POD, SOD, CAT and GR in tomato root, leaf and fruit. (e–g): Glutathione level in tomato root, leaf and fruit. (h): MDA content in tomato root and leaf. Data are taken in triplicate (n = 3) and reported as the mean ± SD. Different lowercase letters on bars indicate the significant differences among treatments at p ≤ 0.05.
Nitrogen, phosphorous, potassium and sulfur contents in tomato leaf. Data in the table are the mean ± SD (n = 3). Different letters indicate significant differences at p ≤ 0.05 among treatments.

Summary of sequencing data and alpha diversity indices of bacterial community (B) and fungal community (F). Data in the table are the mean ± SD (n = 3). Different letters indicate significant differences at p ≤ 0.05 among treatments.

head.ce:title: Alleviation role of functional carbon nanodots for tomato growth and soil environment under drought stress

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The biotoxicity and environmental applications of carbon nanomaterials have always been the focus of research. In this research, functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) show high promotion effects on regulating the growth, development and yield of tomato under drought stress, due to their up-regulation effects on the physiological processes of plants including photosynthesis, antioxidant system, osmotic adjustment, as well as soil amelioration in physicochemical properties and microbial environment during vegetative and reproductive growth stage. The reduction of tissue water content and water use efficiency are moderated by FCNs through improving root vigor and osmolytes (soluble sugar and proline) level, which contributes to maintain the enzyme function, photosynthesis and nutrient uptake in plant. FCNs regulate the enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant system to scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) and inhibit the lipid peroxidation, thus protect the membrane structure and function of plant cells under stress. FCNs up-regulate soil microbial communities under drought stress by regulating the soil pH, enzyme activity, organic carbon and organic matters contents. Our results prove that FCNs are biological friendly to plant growth and soil environment under drought stress, thus exhibit potential as emendator to promote plant tolerance and improve agricultural productivity in water-deficient areas.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is one of the highest priorities in agriculture. However, at least 20% of all developing countries will face water shortages by 2030, making drought tolerance and limited water for irrigation (Mansoor et al., 2021). Agricultural crops are often exposed to abiotic stresses, in which drought stress is reckoned as the most detrimental factor causing crucial loss of crop yield (Osakabe et al., 2014; Tardieu et al., 2014). Drought results in periodic drying and wetting cycles that strongly affects the soil respiration, microbial activity and properties of soil decomposition, further initiating various physiological, biochemical and morphological changes in plants (Geng et al., 2015). As an abiotic stress, drought stress induces the excess generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS), which causes serious oxidative damage to membranes and alters the function of the key metabolic pathways like photosynthesis, mineral uptake and assimilation (Ahanger et al., 2019). Plants up-regulate antioxidant system and osmolytes accumulation to neutralize the excess ROS and maintain the water potential, so that the normal physiological processes of plants are protected (Ahanger et al., 2021). Drought stress shows a severe concussion on the plant whole life cycle by inhibiting seed germination, seedling growth and photosynthesis, membrane functioning, enzyme activity, mineral and water uptake, hence significantly reduces the crop yield (Ahanger et al., 2021). Therefore, it is important for deep understanding of the drought damages on the cash crops as well as seeking ways to improve plant drought tolerance in order to sustain crop production and quality (Kamiar et al., 2021).

Nanotechnology has made a real revolution in the agriculture (Yang, 2018). Based on their potential scope of nanomaterials as nanofertilizers or nanopesticides to deal with biotic or abiotic stresses, their targeted use could produce outstanding results not only to boost crop productivity but also to cope with drought-related issues at soil-plant interface (Kashyap et al., 2017; Ragab and Saad-Allah, 2020). As one biologically-fabricated nanoparticle (NP), biochar can mitigate drought stress in plants through its positive influence on water holding capacity, cation exchange capacity, soil organic carbon and microbial respiration, thus improve nutrient absorption and plant development (Liang et al., 2014; Haider et al., 2015). The application of NPs, such as Fe2O3 NPs, nano-Zn, nano-silica, nano-boron, and ZnO NPs, increased the plant tolerance against the drought stress by activating the antioxidant enzymes, facilitating the nutrient acquisition process, increasing photosynthesis efficiency in plants, and enhancing their growth (Dimkpa et al., 2019; Kamiar et al., 2021). Carbon nanomaterials (CNMs), such as single-walled carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), alleviated the drought stress induced reduction in germination and growth parameter through increasing water uptake, activating plant defense system and reducing electrolyte leakage (Hatami et al., 2017). The fullerene nanoparticles penetrated plant leaf and root tissues, where they binded water in various cell compartments due to their hydroscopic activity (Borišev et al., 2016). Despite the rising researches regarding CNMs application in plant sciences, there are limited studies about the function of carbon nanodots under deficit-water stress condition, particularly during the full life cycle of cash plant.

The water-soluble functional carbon nanodots (FCNs) synthesized from an electrolysis method show outstanding up-regulation effects on plant growth in the whole biological cycle, including germination, vegetative growth and reproductive growth (Chen et al., 2021). The direct effects of FCNs on plants are predominantly ascribed to their chemical structure and properties (Chen et al., 2020a, 2020b). On one hand, the acid and electronegative functional groups on FCNs can decline pH and provide negative charges in medium, to active nutrient ions and biomacromolecules functions. On the other hand, the nanoscale structure and abundant hydrophilic functional groups enable FCNs acting as a transportation to deliver water and nutrient in plant organs, thereby booming plant germination and growth. Besides, FCNs can also increase biomass accumulation and scavenging reactive oxygen species (ROS) by inducing higher root activity, chlorophyll level and antioxidant enzyme activity in plant, resulting in significantly increased resistance of plant under abiotic stress (Chen et al., 2020a, 2020b). Based on their outstanding performance on plant, FCNs may have the ability to mitigate the influence of drought stress on crops and increase productivity of soils in arid area. In this work, the role of FCNs on crop productivity and soil amelioration under severe drought stress is investigated. Tomato, a worldwide vegetable crop within family Solanaceae (Ahanger et al., 2021), is chosen in this study. The present study aims to investigate the effects of FCNs at different concentrations on the vegetative and reproductive growth of tomato under severe drought stress conditions outdoors. Besides, the changes in physicochemical properties and microbial environment of soils with the application of FCNs are also analyzed. As the first study to report the effects of FCNs on crop productivity and soil property under drought stress, it will provide important information for the environmental and biological safety of FCNs. Furthermore, our findings may have important implications for the potential application of FCNs-based materials in promoting crop plant tolerance and improving agricultural productivity in water-deficient areas.

FCNs used in this study were synthesized using only graphite and water by our previous reported electrolysis method (Chen et al., 2020a, 2020b). The FCNs were characterized using several techniques including transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-1011, Japan), Brunner-Emmet-Teller (BET, ASAP 2020, America), Raman spectrometer (inVia, Renishaw, Britain), Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, Bruker Tensor 37, Germany), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Escalab 250Xi, America) and Malvin laser particle size analyzer (Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Britain). FCNs solution stored in fridge at 4 ℃ was filtered and diluted, then added directly into the pot-experiment.

Tomato (Micro Tom) seeds of uniform size were surface sterilized with ethanol (70%) followed by thorough rinsing with distilled water. Trays in greenhouse were supplied with half strength Hoagland solution and regularly monitored until the expansion of the fourth main leaves. The seedlings were then transplanted into pots filled with garden soil and provided adequate irrigation. 10 days after acclimation, FCNs were supplemented in soil with different dosage of 0 mg kg−1, 1 mg kg−1, 3 mg kg−1, 10 mg kg−1 and 30 mg kg−1. All the treatments were exposed to drought stress for 15 days. In the drought condition, the amount of additive water was 25% field moisture capacity (FMC). FMC was determined by saturating 5 kg soils without plants with water, and letting the water drain off completely over time (Dimkpa et al., 2019). The experiments were taken in triplicate. The growth and physiological parameters were statistically analyzed to determine the optimal dosage of FCNs.

To investigate the effect of FCNs on the total life circle of outdoor tomato. The tomato seedlings were transplanted and cultivated in non-drought condition for 10 days, then the further experiments were carried with different groups of treatments included: (a) control-drought (CT-D: only garden soil), (b) FCNs-drought (FCNs-D: 3 mg kg−1 soil), (c) Non-drought (ND: Moist soil without FCNs). In the drought and non-drought groups, the amounts of additive water were 25% and 80% FMC, respectively. Pots were arranged in complete randomized block design with five replicates for each treatment. Plants were cultivated outdoors from 5th April to 20th May in Jinan, Shandong, China, in 2020. Pots were supplied with appropriate shelter in rainy days. The temperatures during culture were provided in the Fig. S1. Biomass, shoot height, root length and the number of the uppermost fully expanded leaves were collected on the 15th day. On the 35th day under drought stress, the numbers of flowers and inflorescences were recorded. The fruits numbers were recorded on the 45th day, as well as photosynthetic and oxidative stress parameters, antioxidant system and osmoregulatory components were analyzed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of the growth and physiological parameters
ce:section.ce:para:
After harvest and rinsing with flowed water, plant height and root length were measured manually. Fresh weight (FW) of plant tissue was weighed after blotting the surface moisture on plant, then dry weight (DW) was measured after blanching at 105ºC for 15 min and drying to constant weight at 75 ℃ by oven. Leaf turgid weight (TW) was recorded after soaking the leaves in distilled water for 12 h at room temperature. Relative water content (RWC) of the seedling was calculated by the Eq. (1):
(1) RWC (%) = (FW - DW)/(TW - DW) × 100

Water uses efficiency (WUE) was calculated from the total dry weight (TDW, g) of plant and total water used (TWU, g) during growing process using the Eq. (2) (Gavilia et al., 2019):
(2) WUE = TDW/TWU

The total chlorophyll in leaves was extracted by ethanol and measured by spectrophotometry at 652 nm using an ultraviolet and visible spectrophotometer (UV, Thermo Evolution 220, America) (Turner, 1981).

To determine the activity of the antioxidant enzymes, fresh plant samples of 0.5 g were crushed with 50 mM phosphate buffer (PBS) of pH 7.8. Then, the mixture was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to get clear supernatant. The indexes such as peroxidase (POD, EC 1.11.1.7), superoxide dismutase (SOD, EC 1.15.1.1), catalase (CAT, EC 1.11.1.6) and glutathione reductase (GR, EC 1.6.4.2) were measured using the reagent kits by UV. SOD activity was determined in xanthine and xanthine oxidase reaction systems using the nitro blue tetrazolium (NBT) method and recorded at 560 nm (Bayer, Fridovich, 1987). CAT activity was assayed by measuring the consumption of H2O2 at 240 nm (Gallego et al., 2012). POD activity was measured by the guaiacol oxidation method at 470 nm (Zhang et al., 1995). GR activity was assayed with the change of the absorbance (Abs.) in 2 min at 340 nm (Fong et al., 1976). Details are given in Supplemented Materials (SMs).

Malondialdehyde (MDA) content was determined in fresh leaves using thiobarbituric acid (Heath, 1968). Oxidized glutathione (GSSG) and reduced glutathione (GSH) contents were estimated with dithionitrobenzoic acid at 412 nm by UV spectrophotometer (Ellman, 1959). Contents of sugars and proline were estimated in dry leaves samples (Fong et al., 1953; Jain and Guruprasad, 1989).

Phosphorus (P) and potassium (K) contents in dry leaves were determined by inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometer (ICP-OES, Agilent 5110, America) after digesting by sulfuric. Nitrogen (N) and sulfur (S) contents were analyzed by organic element analyzer (EA III, Vario MACRO cube, German).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of soil physicochemical parameters and microbial community
ce:section.ce:para: Soil samples were collected after plant harvest. Total nitrogen (TN) in soil was determined by automatic kjeldahl apparatus method. Total phosphorus (TP) and total potassium (TK) were tested by ICP-OES (optima 7000, America) after digestion by hydrofluoric acid- nitric acid- perchloric acid (5:3:1). Dissolved organic matters and organic carbon concentrations were determined with potassium dichromate by volumetric method. Soil pH and electrical conductivity (EC) were determined using 1:5 soil-to-water ratio by a pH meter (HQ11D, America). Cation exchange capacity (CEC) was determined by ammonium chloride-ammonium acetate exchange method. Soil dehydrogenase (DHA) and urease (URE) activities were determined by colorimetric method with UV (Yang et al., 2021). Soil microbial community structure was detected by high-throughput sequencing technology (Yang et al., 2021). The methods are given detailly in SMs.

Statistical analyses were performed with the software package SPSS 19.0 (IBM, Armonk, New York, USA). The data were presented as the mean ± standard deviations (SDs). Differences between treatments were statistically analyzed using one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA). According to Duncan’s Multiple Range Tests, the significant differences (P ≤ 0.05) were labeled with different lowercase letters in all figures and tables.

FCNs used in this study are nanodots of 2–8 nm ( Fig. 1a, b) as measured by TEM. FCNs are easily dissolved in water, and the pH of 1.0 g L−1 FCNs solution is about 2.0. Raman spectrum presented in Fig. 1c shows the two peaks of FCNs namely D- (around 1330 cm−1) and G- (around 1580 cm−1), which is respectively derived from the presence of defects and graphite-like in-plane mode. The ratio of the intensity of D and G peak (ID/IG) is used to assess the crystal purity and disorder concentration of carbon materials. Here, the ID/IG value is 0.80, reflecting the defects resulting from the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface. From XPS survey (Fig. 1e) spectrum, the main elements are carbon and oxygen. FT-IR spectrum, C1s XPS spectrum and O1s XPS spectrum show the main groups of FCNs are carboxyl, ester, hydroxyl, carbonyl and ether bond (Fig. 1d, f, g). Due to the high content of electronegative groups, the Zeta potential of FCNs at pH 2.0 and 7.0 is − 37.8 and − 19.9 (Fig. S2), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs promote tomato vegetative growth under drought stress
ce:section.ce:para: FCNs (from 1 mg kg−1 soils to 30 mg kg−1 soils) amended into soil promote the early growth of tomato and mitigate membrane oxidative damage under drought stress, due to the increased dry weights of seedlings and decreased MDA contents of leaves compared to that without FCNs ( Fig. 2a and i). Specially, additive FCNs of 3 mg kg−1 promote plant early growth and mitigate cell oxidative damage to the greatest extent. FCNs can induce responses of antioxidant system under drought stress by regulating the enzyme activities of antioxidant enzymes (include POD, SOD, CAT, GR in Fig. 2b–e) and levels of other antioxidants (such as GSH in Fig. 2f), which might be due to the adaptive response of the seedlings to drought stress.

Abiotic stresses disturb the cellular redox homeostasis and induce secondary stresses such as osmotic and oxidative stress, which in turn, disturb the balance between generation and removal of ROS (Hatami et al., 2017). Therefore, plants develop enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant systems for scavenging excessive ROS under various stress conditions, and efficient running of these detoxification systems is necessary to cope with oxidative damage in plant cells (Hatami et al., 2017). Nanomaterials significantly improve antioxidant enzyme activities, which is reflected in reduced accumulation of ROS, and greater protection to growth, photosynthesis and key metabolic pathways is attained (Sharma et al., 2019). FCNs of 3 mg kg−1 significantly promote the enzyme activities of POD and CAT, while decrease SOD activity. SOD constitutes the first step of defense machinery against ROS toxicity, and catalyzes the conversion of the superoxide radicals (O2•-) to oxygen (O2) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Mittler, 2002). As H2O2 is still a toxic lipid peroxidant to the plant cells, it should be detoxified by conversion to innocuous H2O in further reactions by POD, CAT and GR (Hatami et al., 2017). Carbon dots (CDs) were demonstrated as scavengers of intracellular ROS (DPPH•, O2 •-, and •OH) to effectively alleviate the oxidative damage of plants (Li et al., 2021). The lower SOD activity along with higher POD and CAT activities further prove that the application of FCNs effectively decrease ROS level, followed with the increase of H2O2 that induces more production of POD and CAT. GSH is a non-enzymatic redox compound that eliminates ROS (O2•- and H2O2) and protects cells against lipid peroxidation (Candan and Tarhan, 2003). GR is insensitive to FCNs of 3 mg kg−1, however, the total GSH (TGSH) contents and GSH/GSSH values increase compared to non-FCNs group (F0, Fig. 2g and h). This reveals that 3 mg kg−1 FCNs can facilitate antioxidation reaction in cells. However, at higher concentrations (10–30 mg kg−1) of FCNs, the POD and CAT activities decrease associating with the increased SOD activity, leading to the accumulation of too much toxic H2O2. Therefore, the high level of FCNs arouses more serious lipid peroxidation and growth inhibition in seedlings under drought stress (Fig. 2a and i). Some carbon nanomaterials at high level were also observed to enhance production of ROS and induce oxidative damage in plant, for example MWCNTs in Oryza sativa and Satureja khuzestanica (Ghorbanpour and Hadian, 2015; Tan et al., 2009), graphene in cabbage, tomato, red spinach and lettuce (Begum et al., 2011), and graphene oxide in Chlorella vulgaris (Hu et al., 2014).

Thus, there is dose-dependent effects of FCNs on regulating antioxidant system of plant under drought stress. As amendment of the soil with 3 mg kg−1 of FCNs shows best improvement effect on plant early growth and antioxidation to cope with drought stress, further experiments about the regulation effects of FCNs on tomato growth and development were conducted with this dosage.

Drought significantly inhibits plant vegetative growth under 15 days of drought stress, which is reflected in the significantly decreased biomass in Fig. 3(b). Drought induces young leaf emergence, but inhibits leaf expansion, biomass accumulation and chlorophyll synthesis (Fig. 3). The suppression of drought on leaf expansion and photosynthetic pigment level leads to the less photosynthetic rate, thereby affecting plant growth and biomass accumulation (Osakabe et al., 2014; Tardieu et al., 2014). In addition, many other factors like plant hydraulic status, osmotic adjustment and antioxidant function are also responsible for reduction in plant growth under drought stress (Khan et al., 2015). Water deficient conditions have a negative impact on the plant defense system. Drought stress disturbs balance between the ROS generation and expense, resulting in membrane lipid peroxidation, electrolyte leakage, and shows severe effects on the plant photosynthetic rate of leaves (Farooq et al., 2009). Drought also results in enhanced lipid peroxidation and antioxidase activities, reflecting in the increased MDA content, as well as the activities of POD, SOD, CAT and GR (Fig. 3g–j).

FCNs can mitigate the negative effects of drought stress on tomato seedlings, according to the increased biomass and chlorophyll level, as well as the reduced MDA content (Fig. 3). Drought stress reduces cellular division and restricts cell cycle transition thereby leading to considerable decline in cell production (Ahanger et al., 2021). However, FCN was proved to be able to accelerate cell division in leaves (Chen et al., 2020a, 2020b), thus improve leaf thickness and photosynthetic pigment level (Fig. S3, Fig. 3e) to enhance photosynthesis. It was also reported that nanomaterials can improve solar energy harvest thereby facilitating chloroplast carbon capture, solar energy harnessing and electron transport, and they also can reduce the ROS within chloroplast and influence the sensing process (Khatri, 2018). CDs were proved to enhance the capacity of plant to interact and optimize the light usage thereby contributing to increased productivity of leaf photosynthesis (Sai et al., 2018). Besides, the chlorophyll content, rubisco activity, electron transfer rate and PSII functioning in leaves can also be improved by CDs, resulting in enhanced photosystem activity and increased crop productivity (Swift et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2018). Therefore, FCNs increase the plant biomass during vegetative growth due to their up-regulation effect on photosynthesis process.

FCNs can significantly improve root activity (Fig. 3). Our previous study has proved that FCNs could facilitate the water uptake in germinant stage, speculating the reason as FCNs penetrating seed coats to create new channels for the entry of water, nutrients and external molecules (Khodakovskay et al., 2012; Tiwari et al., 2014). With the improvement of root absorption capacity to water, FCNs improve the RWC of tomato plant under drought stress (Fig. 3d). Drought stress reduces the RWC in the wheat, but application of FCNs moderates the negative effect caused by stress. It was also demonstrated that CDs can eliminate oxygen free radicals and inhibit the lipid peroxidation reaction, thus reducing the loss of water in plant cells (Li et al., 2021). The higher level of RWC in the plant tissue in turn increases the photosynthesis rate and improves the plant growth under drought stress (Kamiar et al., 2021).

The maintenance of greater tissue water can help to maintain the enzyme function, photosynthesis and mineral up-take in plant (Ahanger et al., 2021). Thus, oxidative damage caused by drought stress is reduced by FCNs. As the first organ responding to drought, roots’ antioxidant enzymes activities are influenced by FCNs addition (Fig. 3). SOD, POD, CAT and GR are key antioxidant enzymes involved in neutralizing the ROS. When the increased tendency of SOD and GR activities are moderated by FCNs compared to those in CT and ND groups, CAT activity in roots under drought stress is significantly improved to 2.1 times by FCNs. In young leaves under drought stress, FCNs improve SOD and CAT activities significantly to stimulate the immune system and remove the excess ROS and H2O2 (Fig. 3). CAT is one of the major H2O2-scavenging enzymes in plants that converts H2O2 into water and molecular oxygen. Here, the elevated CAT activity in the seedlings exposed to FCNs indicates an increased ROS-scavenging capacity, which subsequently protects seedlings from oxidative injuries and also from lipid peroxidation of plasma membrane (Hatami et al., 2017). POD activities in both root and leaf are insensitive to FCNs additive. The reduced ROS (Fig. S4) and lipid peroxidation in leaf (Fig. 3f) indicate higher stability of the cell membrane. The structural stability of the cell membrane is a main factor in protecting plant tissues against osmotic stress (Filek et al., 2012).

Regulation effects of CDs on plants have been described as inducing plants growth, development, photosynthesis, and gene expression in small dose in some researches (Wang et al., 2018). CDs at optimal concentration can increase moisture level by promoting root vigor, enhance photosystem activity by improving the electron transfer rate and pigment content, and alleviate oxidative damage caused by stress by activating the antioxidant system, thus improve carbohydrates content, biomass accumulation and crop yield (Li et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018). Based on above results, FCNs can efficiently promote tomato plant tolerance to drought stress at the vegetative growth stage. On one hand, FCNs improve root activity to absorb more water, thereby promoting RWC in seedlings. On the other hand, the enzymatic and non-enzymatic antioxidant systems are both regulated by FCNs to cope with the ROS burst to mitigate oxidative damage in plant cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs promote tomato reproductive development under drought stress
ce:section.ce:para: Drought stress lead to reduced cell division and growth, reduced photosynthesis, membrane degradation, incompatibility of water and nutrient uptake and transmission, thereby causing serious yield loss of crops (Kamiar et al., 2021). In reproductive stage, the plant height of tomato treated by FCNs under drought stress is higher than CT-D group, and of no significant difference with ND group (Fig. S5). Although drought cause premature flower and fruit drop (Reichardt et al., 2020), the additive of FCNs results in 35.1% higher flower number per inflorescence and 55.6% higher flower number per plant over CD-D group ( Fig. 4a, b, Figs. S6, 7). Fruit yield of tomato decreases significantly under drought stress compared to that in ND group. FCNs amendment increases both fruit number and weight per plant than CT-D and ND (Fig. 4c, d), and dramatically improves the fruit yield by 83.1% and 15.5%, respectively. Besides, FCNs can also increase the soluble sugar content in tomato fruit to improve the fruit flavor (Fig. 4e). In terms of the significantly improved productivity and quality of tomato plant exposed to FCNs under drought stress, FCNs exhibit potential as emendator to increase soil productivity in water-deficient areas.

WUE is an important indicator for assessing the usefulness of the applied water (Kamiar et al., 2021). From Fig. 4(f), deficit water increases WUE by 69.29% compared to the group with full irrigation, which agrees with the previous study (Akhtar et al., 2014). Addition of 3 mg kg−1 FCNs significantly increases WUE by 81.94% and 2.08-folds over that of CT-D and ND, respectively. In the present study, FCNs amendment under drought stress increases the fruit yield, resulting in the improvement of WUE.

On one hand, the inhibitory impact of water stress could be a consequence of an osmotic barrier that decreases the water absorbing ability of plant. Enhanced accumulation of compatible osmolytes significantly influence the growth and yield of crop plants. Proline and water-soluble sugar are two important osmolytes in plant. Under drought stress, FCNs increase the osmotic and aqueous potential of the plant through activating the accumulation of the soluble sugar (Fig. 4e) and proline (Fig. S8), therefore contributing to the higher WUE and RWC. On the other hand, due to the ability of binding water molecules, carbon nanomaterials, such as fullerenes, were proved to act as intracellular binders of water, creating an additional water reserve, and enabling adaptation to drought stress (Borišev et al., 2016). Our previous study also revealed that FCNs can act as a carrier to transport cargoes, such as water, nutrients and ions, in the organs of plant (Chen et al., 2020a, 2020b). Therefore, in the present study, FCNs mediating the increase in osmolytes and root vigor may contribute to better growth and yield production of tomato.

Drought results in considerable oxidative damage through enhancement in the ROS accumulation, lipid peroxidation and electrolyte leakage (Ahanger et al., 2021). In the reproductive period of tomato under long-term drought stress, application of FCNs results in significant enhancement in the functioning of antioxidant system in root by up-regulating the activities of SOD, CAT, GR, and the non-enzymatic component ( Fig. 5). Compared with the control group, FCNs increase SOD by 58.7%, CAT by 20.8%, GR by 82.4% and GSH by 92.8% in roots. Although FCNs increase CAT activity, they decrease SOD activity and the total GSH content in leaves and fruits. This indicates that the dominating oxidative damage in leaves and fruits during the reproductive growth stage results from H2O2, thereby inducing the up-regulation of CAT activity. Increased activity of antioxidant enzymes has been reported to prevent drought mediated ROS triggering decline in photosynthesis by improving membrane stability through quick elimination of ROS (Yang et al., 2014). Due to the regulation effects of FCNs on antioxidant system, the MDA contents of both root and leaf decline dramatically in FCNs-D group compared to CT-D group. This suggests that FCNs can reduce the oxidative damage by declining lipid peroxidation, protecting membrane stability and up-regulating the antioxidant system. Up-regulation of the antioxidant system due to FCNs supplementation results in amelioration of the drought mediated decline in growth and photosynthesis significantly.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FCNs influence nutrient acquisition by tomato under drought stress
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Table 1, drought declines P, K and S content in leaves by 67.9%, 38.6% and 6.0%, respectively, over the non-drought group, however, the N content is improved by 76.8%. The increase of N content may be ascribed to the improved antioxidant enzyme system. With the application of FCNs under drought stress, the P, K and S content in leaf is raised by 2.1-fold, 57.3% and 23.4%, respectively. Based on the aromatic structure and rich electronegative functional groups, FCNs increase the availability of nutrient via cation exchange, surface interactions and water availability processes (Wei et al., 2020). Increased growth and tolerance in FCNs treated seedlings can be due to the presence of key elements including N, K and S, which can strengthen the function of antioxidant system (such as antioxidant enzymes and GSH), regulate photosynthesis (chlorophyll synthesis and CO2 exchange) and enhance osmolyte synthesis to enhance the ROS scavenging system, organics synthesis and tissue water content (Ahanger et al., 2021, 2019; Dimkpa et al., 2019). FCNs also improve reproductive growth and yield in tomato through increased uptake of P and K. Therefore, FCNs amendment can significantly moderate the negative effect of drought on tomato growth and development. Based on the above, improvements in the plant growth and yield by the FCNs amendment are due to their participation in increasing the enzymatic activity, improving the membrane structure stability, inducing cell division, promoting water and nutrient uptake, so that they promote the drought tolerance of tomato during both vegetative and reproductive growth stage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of FCNs on physicochemical properties of soils
ce:section.ce:para: The impacts of FCNs on the physical, chemical and enzyme activities of soils are shown in Table S1. By the addition of FCNs, the organic carbon and organic matters increase while the P content and pH decreased. The decreased P content may be result from the uptake by plant. The DHA (related to the bio-hydrolysis of organic compounds) activity in soils treated with FCNs is increased to more than twice than CT under drought stress, due to the higher content of organic matters in FCNs treated soils and the electronic supply capability of FCNs. The increased DHA activity represents the improved soil microbial activity of viable microbial cells (Shrestha et al., 2013). However, FCNs have no significant impacts on the URE (related to organic N transformation) activity.

The enhancement of organic matters in soil increase the activity of antioxidant enzymes, as well as make favorable environment for microorganisms to stimulate soil microbial communities (Haider et al., 2020). The rich microbial communities in turn result in greater accumulation of carbon in soil through various metabolic activities of microbial communities (Haider et al., 2020). The above results indicate that FCNs are friendly to soil enzymatic and microbial environment. Therefore, FCNs can be used as a conditioner coupling with plants to boost up soil carbon sequestration potential and enhance soil quality under drought stress, especially on alkaline soil.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of FCNs on soil microbial environment
ce:section.ce:para: Impacts of drought on soil microorganisms include direct effects through physical osmotic stress and changes in microbial secondary metabolite production, as well as indirect effects through altered nutrient availability, and changes in redox and pH (Wang et al., 2020). The composition of soil microbial communities responds faster than the total microbial biomass to soil moisture stress, due to selective enrichment or repression of specific groups under those conditions resulting from differential moisture stress responses of different types of microbes (Wang et al., 2020).

By high throughput sequencing, approximately 10,989 observed OTUs of bacterial community are yielded, and 4.65% OTUs are shared by three groups (Fig. S9a). The number of bacterial sequences in FCNs treatment is higher than those in other treatments. In each soil sample, the Good’s coverage of bacterial sequences is above 97%, indicating that the libraries might truly reflect the sequencing depth of soil bacterial communities ( Table 2). The Chao1 index of bacterial sequences increases with the addition of FCNs in dry soil, which is similar with that in moist soil. However, the Shannon and Simpson indices of bacterial sequences in all treatments of soils are of no significant differences (Table 2). The Chao1 index represents for species richness, while Shannon metric indicates the abundance of observed OTU which accounts for both richness and evenness (Kim et al., 2017). Therefore, FCNs can increase the bacterial community diversity, instead of negatively affecting soil bacteria in soil under drought stress.

Approximately 753 observed OTUs of fungal community are yielded, and 13.28% OTUs are shared by three groups (Fig. S9b). The similar Chao1 index of fungal sequences reflects the differences in community richness among the three groups are insignificant. Both of the Shannon and Simpson indices of fungal sequences increase in FCNs-D and ND groups, indicating the increased diversity of the fungus community in soils treated by FCNs under drought stress (Table 2). The changes in community biodiversity could be attributed to the plant growth and water content.

The phylogenetic classification of sequence tags in phyla, classes, orders, families and genera of bacterial community are listed in Table 2. According to the results of OTU species annotation, twenty dominant relative abundances of soil bacteria at the phylum level are shown in Fig. S10a. Actinobacteria and Proteobacteria are the two predominant phyla, covering about 75% of the classified sequences. In addition, Actinobacteria is more tolerant under drought stress as drought significantly increased its abundance, while Proteobacteria and Acidobacteria are negatively affected by drought (Su et al., 2020). FCNs increase the relative abundance of Actinobacteria, Proteobacteria and Acidobacteria in arid soil, compared to CT. However, the relative abundance of Actinobacteria in non-arid soil is lower than FCNs-D group, while the Acidobacteria exhibits the opposite tendency. At the genus level, the relative abundance of Streptomyces, Lechevalieria and Cetobacterium are dramatically increase under drought stress, revealing they are more tolerant genera (Fig. S11a). However, FCNs obviously suppress the up-regulation of Lechevalieria and Cetobacterium, while further increase the relative abundance of Streptomyces. As shown in Fig. S12a, the heat map of the top 50 soil bacterial genera in each soil sample is constructed in order to identify the similarities and differences of bacterial community structures. FCNs treated dry soil and normal water treated soil are clustered close to one another, which implies similarities between bacterial communities.

The dominant relative abundances of twenty phyla of soil fungi are shown in Fig. S10b. For fungi, Ascomycota has the highest relative abundance in soil samples, accounting for more than 75%, and the followings are Mortierellomycota and Basidiomycota. The relative abundances of Mortierellomycota and Basidiomycota increase with the addition of FCNs and water, while that of Ascomycota decrease (Fig. S10b). FCNs also moderate the change in genus level of fungi (Fig. S11b). As shown in the heat map of the top 50 soil fungal genera (Fig. S12b), FCNs treated arid soil and normal water treated soil are clustered close to one another, which implies similarities between fungal communities.

Increased abundances of the microbial taxa are possibly due to the presence of rich and diverse organic matters in soils (Su et al., 2020). The increased relative abundance of Proteobacteria indicates that FCNs can improve the soil environment, as the growth of Proteobacteria heavily depends on nutrient availability and is in general sensitive to environmental perturbations (Bouskill et al., 2013). Plant growth-promoting rhizobacteria have remarkable positive effects on plant productivity and biotic or abiotic stress tolerance (He et al., 2021). Actinobacterium can upregulate the osmoregulation and antioxidant defense metabolism, improve the growth and photosynthesis of plant, thus enhance the drought tolerance in maize (Selim et al., 2019). As a member of Actinobacteria, Streptomyces can also alleviate drought stress in tomato plants, and promote fruit yield (Sakineh et al., 2020). FCNs also suppress the changes in fungi genera level ascribed to the drought, which is in accordance with that of bacteria. FCNs improve the abundances of Basidiomycota to activate the glutathione-ascorbate cycle, which can elevate the anti-oxidative capacity to enhance plant tolerance and yield under stress (Waller et al., 2005). Fungi in the soil can also absorb more water through hyphae and transfer water to the plant, thus enhance plant tolerance to drought stress (Smith and Smith, 2011). Therefore, FCNs can regulate the relative abundance of bacteria and fungi of arid soils to bring them close to the soils with normal water content, although decrease the total fungi abundance at the phylum level. Although some CNMs have been reported to negatively affect the bacterial diversity of soils (Shrestha et al., 2013), our results indicate that FCNs up-regulate soil bacterial communities. The results reveal that FCNs addition has an important effect on the richness, diversity, and structure of soil bacterial and fungal communities under drought stress, instead of destroying the micro-ecological environment of soils. On one hand, FCNs induce the alterations in soil microbial community structure by regulating the soil pH, enzyme activity, organic carbon and organic matters contents (Zhang et al., 2020). The higher nutrient availability due to FCNs amendment allow relatively rapid growth of microbial groups. On the other hand, FCNs act as a ROS scavenger to eliminate the excess ROS aroused by drought, thus protect the integrity and stability of the membrane structure in microbial cells (Li et al., 2021).

In summary, FCNs improve the growth, development and yield of tomato, and minimize the negative effects of drought stress on plant under severe drought stress. Application of FCNs activates some physiological processes of plant, such as chlorophyll formation, cell division, enzymatic activation, osmolyte synthesis, as well as water and nutrient uptake, then mitigates the reduction of dry matter and fruit yield aroused by drought stress. Besides, FCNs improve soil properties like pH, organic matter, organic carbon as well as soil enzyme activity and microbial community, which also contributes significantly to tomato growth to counteract the drought mediated damage. Generally, our study provides new insight into the application of appropriate level of FCNs to manage drought tolerance of valuable crops and increase crop yield in water-deficient area.

Qiong Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xiufeng Cao: Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Xiangkun Nie: Software, Formal analysis. Yuanyuan Li: Data curation, Formal analysis. Taibo Liang: Validation, Resources. Lijie Ci: Project administration, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.
